item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are you sure? Unless my maths is off, a 300 km radius circle is about 280,000 square km, or less than half the size of Ukraine.

While that sounds like a lot, the site is most likely in a remote area of the Russian Far East, and it should ideally be large enough to exclude most radar systems. I don't know enough about air traffic control to know if this would be a hugely disruptive zone, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One nitpick: a no-fly zone of 300 km from the containment site means an area roughly the size of Ukraine, or most of the area in the Four Corners States.

It is, otherwise, a great idea and done very well. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really, really like the bit at the end about the recessive nature of the anomaly. Recessive mutations reach fixation much slower than dominant or codominant mutations, but increase the most steeply when they finally gain enough "critical mass," so to speak. One of my favorite instances of Paretto curves. Honestly, why not explore that a bit? You could add some diagrams going into the evolutionary mechanics of allele fixation and help folks understand WHY this is keter-class.

Easy +1 for decent concept and great execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good, simple, rather amusing little article, but the research notes are needless word bloat and don't add anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, much appreciated, although trying to meet this standard is now very frightening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're beginning to inch your way up my list of favorite authors, General. You're consistently dropping these amazing works that evoke great emotions within me. This is no exception.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed it. I cut an explanation of which radar sees what for brevity, but since its omission was noticeable I added it back in. I prefer having each radar see its own missile only, for maximum confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you liked it. I also found the ending addendum a bit of a necessary evil, but my reasoning was more because this would be relatively easily found and contained without something extra, and I wanted to keep an MTF roaming all over the Pacific constantly just to hunt birds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This blows a vaguely similar idea I once had out of the water. This is a really neat anomaly for a species to have. :D +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually think this is delightful. It asks an interesting question about the nature of anomalous entities in the collective consciousness and then answers it with:

It is galling to have made such an accomplishment out of reach of all modern science and receive no recognition for it

Which puts me in the mind of Vezaz' What The Spybird Saw in its connection between the anomalous and the mundane. The only complaint I'd have is in the nature of the bomb-shaped droppings. They're identified as appearing to fly towards the radar, but do they fly towards multiple radars if there are more than one? Does each radar see one coming towards itself and the others, or do they each just see their own? A minor quibble, either way.

Come to think of it, given the nature of those poops it's more than reasonable that this would receive an upgrade to Keter. If every time one of these birds takes a shit the nearest radar operator thinks he's about to get nuked by an ole-timey Soviet missile, I'd be worried about an international panic too. I've seen birds. Birds shit a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If your image isn't working, open the file directly and refresh the page. A lot of times it's an image cache issue and wikidot doesn't have a fix for it. If you reload the page yourself it should be kosher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. It's not stellar by any means, but there's so little wrong with it that the rest is upvote-worthy for sure. The core anomaly is engaging and interesting and, hell, even funny — bird poops being mistaken for missiles is something I never thought I'd live to see, and you write it in-depth without being boring, something that's often a fine line to tread. The backstory was short and sweet, and left me feeling satisfied with the narrative. The upgrade to Keter felt a little forced on my first read, but I can't think how you'd end it better, so it's a necessary evil :P

Overall, very good work making an anomaly interesting without hundreds of addenda and collapsibles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the radar-jamming birbs. The detail about their poops having the same radar signatures as bombs was especially amusing. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image is working alright on my device.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image source.

Uh, for some reason the image isn't working and I have no idea why. Anybody have any ideas? nevermind, it shows now.
